intent: Cross-version replay of rocky9-broad-interface-bait against el10 policyvers 35. logrotate_t -> shadow_t:file { read, getattr }. Verifies that audit2allow -R still picks auth_read_shadow() on el10 (selinux-policy-42), and that the TeAllowable bucket classification is stable. Both el9 and el10 audit2why return 'Unknown - should be dontaudit'd' for this access (dontaudit rule present in both selinux-policy 38 and 42); audit2allow -N and -R still emit the allow.
_audit2why_note: audit2why returns 'Unknown - should be dontaudit'd by active policy' on BOTH el9 and el10. The dontaudit rule is present in both policy versions. audit2allow -N correctly emits the raw allow (logrotate_t shadow_t:file { getattr read }); audit2allow -R still uses auth_read_shadow() on el10. The bucket is stable.

oracle.audit2why_category: Unknown - should be dontaudit'd (identical to el9 oracle output for both records)
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: Same as el9: auth_read_shadow() expands to typeattribute can_read_shadow_passwords + open/ioctl/lock padding + etc_t:dir grant. Our expected.te: allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file { getattr read }; - exact 2 denied perms only.

xver_baseline._note: Both el9 and el10 audit2why return identical 'Unknown/dontaudit' text. The auth_read_shadow() macro is present in selinux-policy-42/el10 with the same over-broadening profile as el9.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: An impl using audit2allow -R on el10 still gets auth_read_shadow(logrotate_t) - same over-broadening macro, same profile on selinux-policy-42 as on el9 38.
